items.q: Roughly what proportion of energy passes from one trophic level to the next?
items.why: Students assume energy is conserved across levels. Most energy is lost as heat (respiration), through movement, indigestible parts, and uneaten material. The 10% rule explains why food chains are short.

items.q: What process moves carbon from the atmosphere into plants?
items.why: Students confuse the directions. Photosynthesis takes CO2 OUT of the atmosphere (plants build sugars); respiration RELEASES CO2. Both run simultaneously in plants, but only photosynthesis fixes new carbon.

items.q: How does the Brundtland Report define sustainable development?
items.correct: Meeting present needs without compromising future generations' ability to meet theirs.
items.why: Students treat sustainability as 'no growth'. The Brundtland definition explicitly balances development AND environmental concerns - it's about INTERGENERATIONAL equity, not freezing change.

items.q: The ecological footprint concept was introduced by Wackernagel and Rees in:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1996. “1972” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: Date annual biocapacity is exhausted. “Maximum population” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Year ecological footprint concept was published?
items.why: The real figure is 1996 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
